summary: Hotz refuses to speak with the New York Times, citing a complete loss of respect for legacy media institutions. He traces his shift in attitude from respect (pre-2012) to indifference and even contempt, arguing that the institutions once considered powerful no longer hold sway over the people he respects. He expresses optimism about decentralized alternatives—crypto, diverse internet communities, and independent thinkers—as the foundations of a new, more competent order that will eventually emerge.
key_points: Legacy media institutions like the NYT have lost their status and power among those Hotz respects
The shift away from respecting mainstream institutions happened roughly between 2012-2017
Crypto and decentralized communities represent a path to 'real entrepreneurship' free from traditional gatekeepers
Censorship on mainstream platforms is pushing 'normal' people toward alternatives, which Hotz sees as positive
The smartest people working on the future won't be found on any establishment's lists
money_quotes: I do not respect the New York Times. They have no status or power to convey to me.
We all know we all know the emperor has no clothes.
There's a future for real entrepreneurship free from the fake VC San Francisco storybook.
The censorship has gotten to the point 'normal' people are leaving, not just witches.
A diverse Internet is a good Internet.
I want a government I'd be proud to work under, because they radiate competence.
I want to live in a society that aspires to greatness.
I will follow the regime that delivers a satisfying rebuttal to the Unabomber Manifesto.
key_insight: The old institutions—media, VC, government—have lost their legitimacy among the people actually building the future, and a new decentralized order is quietly emerging to replace them.
